coredata.dc:title: Selective and efficient adsorption of boron (III) from water by 3D porous CQDs/LDHs with oxygen-rich functional groups
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Fanqing
Ma, Wei
Wu, Lei
Hao, Hongxia
Xin, Liu
Chen, Zhen
Wang, Moyan
coredata.dc:description: The 3D porous CQDs/LDHs have been synthesized using CQDs as structure-directing agent to adsorb boron(III) from aqueous solution. Characteristic results showed that the prepared CQDs/LDHs have high adsorption capacity and selectivity. FTIR and XPS spectra revealed that CQDs/LDHs possessed more oxygen-containing groups than LDHs. BET data revealed that CQDs/LDHs had a specific surface area of 186 m2/g, which is twice larger than LDHs of 91.6 m2/g. Effects of the temperature, pH and boron(III) concentration on adsorption performance of boron(III) were investigated. The results showed that the maximum adsorption capacity of 19.5 mg/g on CQDs/LDHs was obtained when the initial boron(III) concentration is 20 mg/L at pH 8.5. In the competitive adsorption system, the removal rate of CQDs/LDHs toward boron(III) can reach to 89.7%, which is three times bigger than raw LDHs. Adsorption isotherm and kinetics studies revealed that boron(III) adsorption process was well-described by Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetic model, respectively. Adsorption and characterization data proved that the adsorption mechanism of boron(III) is a complex process, including anion exchange and oxygen-containing group adsorption. This work not only gives a new way to synthesize 3D porous material but also provides a potential material to selectively adsorp boron(III).
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
3D porous structure
Boron
Selective adsorption
Oxygen-rich groups

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared initial LDHs and 3 wt% CQDs/LDHs composite nanosheets.
SEM micrographs of (a) initial LDHs and (b, c) 3D porous CQDs/LDHs.
TEM micrographs of surface of (a, b) initial LDHs, (c, d) 3D porous CQDs/LDHs.
FT–IR spectra of the boron-free CQDs/LDHs and boron adsorbed on CQDs/LDHs.

(a) The adsorption capacity of boron by the CQDs/LDHs nanosheets and internal LDHs material. CQDs/LDHs composite, 2 mg; sample solution (volume), 100 mL; boron concentration, 25 mg/L, pH = 8.5; (b) The concentration of boron (III), SO4 2−, CO3 2−, PO4 3− and Cl− before adsorption process and after adsorption by CQDs/LDHs and LDHs. (c) The adsorption capacity of boron by the CQDs/LDHs nanosheets with different contents of CQDs; CQDs/LDHs composite, 2 mg; sample solution (volume), 100 mL; boron concentration, 25 mg/L, pH = 8.5; (d) The effect of the solution pH on the adsorption of boron by CQDs/LDHs. CQDs/LDHs composite, 2 mg; sample solution (volume), 100 mL; boron concentration, 25 mg/L.
Zeta potential distribution of B free and B adsorbed CQDs/LDHs with respect to the solution pH.
Adsorption isotherm of boron on CQDs/LDHs at pH = 8.5 and desorption equilibrium data at pH = 4.

(a) Langmuir and Freundlich model plot at various pH for boron removal using CQDs/LDHs composite at room temperature; (b) Relationship of ln (q/c) versus 1/T and estimated enthalpy change (∆H) as the function of adsorbed concentration for boron; (c) Arrhenius plots of the apparent rate constants for B removal by a suspension of CQDs/LDHs in H3BO3 solution with an initial pH of 8.5; (d) Comparison between boron and nitrate ion the adsorption solution.
Photoluminescence spectra of the LDHs, boron free CQDs/LDHs and boron adsorbed CQDs/LDHs under excitation wavelength of 360 nm.
XPS spectra of free CQDs/LDHs, boron adsorbed CQDs/LDHs and high-resolution XPS spectra in C 1s of boron-free CQDs/LDHs and boron adsorbed on CQDs/LDHs.
Chemical composition of LDHs and CQDs/LDHs.
BET properties of LDHs and CQDs/LDHs.
The comparison of maximum boron (III) adsorption capacity of various adsorbents.
Distribution coefficient and selectivity coefficient of CQDs/LDHs.
Langmuir isotherm constants for boron adsorption by CQDs/LDHs composite at various pH.

q e
=

)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the isotherm and kinetics models reaction constants of boron sorption by CQDs/LDHs composite with experimental data.

head.ce:title: Selective and efficient adsorption of boron (III) from water by 3D porous CQDs/LDHs with oxygen-rich functional groups

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The 3D porous CQDs/LDHs have been synthesized using CQDs as structure-directing agent to adsorb boron(III) from aqueous solution. Characteristic results showed that the prepared CQDs/LDHs have high adsorption capacity and selectivity. FTIR and XPS spectra revealed that CQDs/LDHs possessed more oxygen-containing groups than LDHs. BET data revealed that CQDs/LDHs had a specific surface area of 186 m2/g, which is twice larger than LDHs of 91.6 m2/g. Effects of the temperature, pH and boron(III) concentration on adsorption performance of boron(III) were investigated. The results showed that the maximum adsorption capacity of 19.5 mg/g on CQDs/LDHs was obtained when the initial boron(III) concentration is 20 mg/L at pH 8.5. In the competitive adsorption system, the removal rate of CQDs/LDHs toward boron(III) can reach to 89.7%, which is three times bigger than raw LDHs. Adsorption isotherm and kinetics studies revealed that boron(III) adsorption process was well-described by Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetic model, respectively. Adsorption and characterization data proved that the adsorption mechanism of boron(III) is a complex process, including anion exchange and oxygen-containing group adsorption. This work not only gives a new way to synthesize 3D porous material but also provides a potential material to selectively adsorp boron(III).

ce:section.ce:para: Boron as an essential micronutrient for humans, animals and some plants is widely distributed in the world [1]. For example, boric acid and borate are used as raw materials for the manufacture of glass, soap, detergents, flame retardants and as a neutron absorbent for nuclear installations [2]. Therefore, the waste of the boron became a serious problem because of the natural and anthropogenic reasons. During the use of these boron products, some boron is discharged to the environment. Those borons may be deposited in the soil and absorbed by plants or flowed into rivers to pollute the drinking water sources, and leads to a series of environment and health problems [3]. According to the current World Health Organization guidelines on drinking water quality, the recommended boron content in drinking water is established as 2.4 mg/L [4]. Many researches show that drinking water with high content of boron (III) over a long period of time can lead to problems with digestion, circulatory system and nervous system. Meanwhile, boron is also a valuable resource in the salt lake brine which needs more-effective method to separate from the complex solution.

In recent years, removal of boron from an aqueous solution can be obtained by various techniques, such as chemical precipitation, adsorption, electro-dialysis and membrane filtration [5,6]. Among these methods, adsorption is the most popular and the most promising method in boron removal process, mainly because it requires simple work conditions and can be used to water containing low levels of boron [7]. Investigation of boron adsorption is being conducted on many different absorbents, for example: natural polymer, synthesized clay materials, fly ash, metal oxides [8], cellulose [9], activated carbons and ion-exchange resins [10]. However, all of these adsorbents had low adsorption capacities for boron (III). As literature shows, the activated alumina, natural clay bentonite, illite and sepiolite can be used for the removal of boron (III), but these adsorbents presented very low adsorption capacities [11,12].

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have been studied as potential adsorbents for the uptake of various boron (III) species. LDHs are a group of clay minerals with a similar structure from both waste products and abundant deposits in nature [13-15]. LDHs have lots of divalent cations, and these cations can be replaced by trivalent cations, and cause a net positive charge, which can be changed with anions in the layered structure [16]. The positive charge can also be balanced by other anions from solution and be used as an anion exchanger. Furthermore, LDHs also be called an anionic clay by researchers because it has layered structure and has anion exchange capacity

The adsorption of boron (III) on the LDHs materials was deeply affected by the double layered structure, surface area and the functional groups of the LDHs. Several authors have investigated the adsorption of boron (III) by the modified LDHs. For example, gluconate-intercalated Mg-Al LDH and cellulose-supported Mg-Al LDH were both synthesized to improve the boron (III) adsorption capacity [17,18]. In particular, the results show that the introduced functional groups can be used as adsorption sites for the formation of bis-chelate and monodentate complexes with borate [19]. In the most previous works, the modified LDHs only revealed higher adsorption capacity on boron (III), however, the selective adsorption of boron (III) on modified LDHs has never been reported [20,21]. Biomass is known as a new environmental and low-cost organic materials which have been used in many field recently because of their superior properties such as abundant functional groups, water solubility and good biocompatibility [22, 23]. As far as the authors know, no studies have reported using the biomass-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as structure-directing agent to modify the LDHs.

In this study, a novel 3D porous CQDs/LDHs has been synthesized by using the CQDs as the structure-directing agent. The synthesized 3D porous CQDs/LDHs were used to selectively remove boron (III) in the mix aqueous solution. The effects of the solution pH, temperature, CQDs loading content and coexisting inorganic ions on the adsorption capacity of boron (III) were investigated. In addition, the adsorption mechanism of boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs was investigated by the adsorption and desorption experiments in combination with thermodynamic calculation and characterization process. This study provides a versatile method for high efficiency 3D porous CQDs/LDHs production, which can be extended to other material modified process.

Fresh Orange Peel was prepared and used within 2 weeks of creation for all syntheses. Fruits were first rinsed thoroughly using DDW. Colored peels were carefully cut away from the fruit and then further cut into small pieces and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Other reagents used in this work were of analytical grade and purchased from Dalian (China). All the reagents used in the study were analytical grade, and all solutions were prepared with double distilled water (DDW).

After drying, 2 g OP powders were dispersed into 12 mL of DDW using ultrasonication, for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred into 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, the autoclave was heated at 140 °C for 2 h under autogenous pressure and cooled down to room temperature. Then, the dispersions were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and the supernatants were filtered through 0.20 µm syringe filters. The CQDs dispersions were collected, freeze-dried and the light-brown residues obtained were stored in dark, until use.

The preparation method for LDHs was referenced from Jiang et al. [14], typically, Al(NO3)3·9H2O (3.09 g), Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (4.29 g) were dissolved in 50 mL DDW to form a clear solution. The solution was then slowly transferred into the 50 mL NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. Then, the mix solution was stirred for 2 h. Then, the mixture was aged for 24 h at 65 °C and the precipitate was filtered through 0.20 µm syringe filters. The products were washed with DDW several times until pH reached 6–7. The solids were dried at 60 °C for 24 h and sieved to a size below 0.140 mm for further experiments.

The CQDs modified LDHs were prepared through a facile in situ synthesis process. In brief, Al(NO3)3·9H2O (3.09 g), Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (4.29 g) were dissolved in 50 mL DDW to form a clear solution. Then, different concentrations (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 ppm) of CQDs were added to the mix solution. The other procedures followed the same LDHs synthesis procedures. The synthesized CQDs modified LDHs with different CQDs loading content at 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 ppm were named as CQDs/LDHs-0.5, CQDs/LDHs-1, CQDs/LDHs-2, CQDs/LDHs, CQDs/LDHs-4 and CQDs/LDHs-5.

Stock solution (1000 mg/L) of boron (III) was prepared by dissolving boric acid into DDW. Batch removal studies were carried out at the desired adsorption temperatures and the initial concentrations ranging from 5 mg/L to 25 mg/L. An aliquot of adsorbent (100 mg) was directly added into 20 mg/L boron solution (100 mL) subjected to orbital shaking for 3 h at 300 rpm. Solution pH controlled with HCl (1 mol/L) or NaOH (1 mol/L) which has an important influence on the adsorption process. The mixed solution was synthesized by sodium salt with the concentration of 20 mg/L (Na2SO4, Na2CO3, Na3PO4 and NaCl). At the end of adsorption, the solution was centrifuged and filtered by 0.2 µm syringe filters. Finally, the filtrate was analyzed for residual boron (III) using inductively coupled plasma with Ar plasma of 1000 W at 12 L/min flow rate. Meinhard nebulizer was used at flow rate of 1 L/min. The wavelength of boron (III) was at 249.8 nm. (Horiba Jobin Yvon, ICP-AES JY2000).

Each adsorption experiment above was conducted twice to obtain reproductive results with error < 5%. In the case of deviation larger than 5%, more tests were carried out. All the adsorption experiments data listed in adsorption sections are the average values of two tests.

Desorption ability of the boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs was investigated by desorption experiment. First, the adsorption experiment was carried out as described in the previous section, once equilibrium was attained, the adsorbent was removed from boron (III) solution by centrifugation. The boron (III) adsorbed adsorbent was then contacted with 100 mL of a solution without boron (III) and at a given pH or a solution of 0.1 M NaOH (the pH was conducted by the 0.01 M HNO3 or NaOH solution). After 3 days, the solution was analyzed to determine the concentration of boron (III) for desorption equilibrium. The amount of boron (III) that remained adsorbed was evaluated by performing a boron (III) mass balance, which can be mathematically expressed as follows:
(1)
q d
=
q 0
−
V i
m
C d

where C 0 (mg/g) is the concentration of boron (III) at the adsorption equilibrium; q 0 (mg/g) is the mass of boron (III) adsorbed on CQDs/LDHs at the beginning of the desorption experiment; qd (mg/g) is the mass of boron (III) that remained adsorbed on CQDs/LDHs at the end of the desorption experiment, and Vi is the volume of the solution without boron (III) added to the desorption experiment.

The synthesized LDHs and CQDs/LDHs-3 were dissolved in 1 M HNO3, and the content of Mg and Al was detected by an atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS Perkin Elmer instrument co., LTD: AAnalyst700). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the OP-R and three HF-D were recorded using Rigaku: D/Max 2400 with a GADDS powder X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) source at 40 kV and 40 mA over a range of 2θ angles from 5 ° to 80 °. FTIR spectra of three HF-D before and after adsorption of arsenic was recorded within the range of 4000–400 cm−1 with KBr as the matrix by using a Bruker MPA FT–IR spectrophotometer. For electron microscopy measurements, the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, 200 keV, JEOL, JEM-2100f) and the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, America, FEI: NOVA Nano SEM 450) were used. The As, Fe and O components of As (V) free and As (V) loaded 10 wt% HF-D were further identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher: ESCALAB 250Xi using mono Al Kα as the photoexcitation source). C 1s (CC bond) was calibrated at 284.5 eV. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were recorded on an Edinburgh FI/FSTCSPC 920 spectrophotometer.

Chemical analysis of the LDHs and CQDs/LDHs is given in Table 1 . As the data shows, the Mg/Al molar ratios of the prepared LDHs and CQDs/LDHs were 2.97 and 2.93, respectively. These values are close to the Mg/Al molar ratio of the precursors used in the synthesis process, which indicates that most of the Mg2+ and Al3+ in the solution are precipitated as the LDHs.

The X-ray diffraction of the LDHs, boron (III) free CQDs/LDHs and boron (III) loaded CQDs/LDHs (XRD) was performed in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of the prepared CQDs/LDHs only shows the typical diffraction peaks of LDHs. There is no extra characteristic peak observed in the CQDs/LDHs, indicating that there have been no impurities in the as-prepared hybrid. The XRD pattern of various LDHs exhibits a sharp and symmetric basal (0 0 3), (0 0 6) and (0 1 2) reflections at 2θ values, which are typical of a crystalline LDH structure (JCPDS 22-0700) [24]. As shown in the XRD patterns of the LDHs and CQDs/LDHs, the low intensities and width of the peaks suggest a small crystal size. However, the characteristic peak for CQDs at 26 ° is too weak to be observed due to its lower concentration (Fig. S1).

Details about the morphology of the LDHs and CQDs/LDHs were examined in Fig. 2 . The irregular layered structure has been found in raw LDHs. However, when CQDs is introduced, the CQDs/LDHs displayed ultrathin nanosheets interconnected and assembly of hierarchical 3D porous microspheres, showing that CQDs have a significant effect on the shape of the sample. This also has been proved by the TEM and HRTEM images of the prepared materials. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the raw LDHs displayed a regular layer with integrity crystal structure which is consistent with the SEM images and XRD patterns. Once the CQDs participated, the CQDs/LDHs not only revealed 3D porous hollow microspheres but also showed much thinner thickness of the nanosheets (Fig. 3c and d). It is suggesting that 3D porous CQDs/LDHs may expose more active sites. TEM image of CQDs in Fig. S2 reveals their spherical shape with a diameter of 4–10 nm and the HRTEM displays a lattice fringe of 0.22 nm, which is assigned to the (100) planes of CQDs. The lattice spacing of 0.22 nm is in accordance with the (100) plane of CQDs (Fig. 3d) which is also shown in Fig. S2, indicating the CQDs have been successfully incorporated in the CQDs/LDHs. In addition, once in the presence of CQDs, the CQDs/LDHs became poorly crystalline due to the less ordered structures as usually observed for small nanoparticles [26]. These results indicate that this method provides an effective, simple and general way to get 3D porous hierarchical nanomaterials.

The BET data of the LDHs and CQDs/LDHs are presented in Table 2 . As the data show, specific surface area of CQDs/LDHs is determined to be 186 m2/g, which is significantly higher than raw LDHs (91.6 m2/g). It might be because of that the introduction of CQDs could improve the dispersion ability of the LDHs and become the in-situ locus of the crystal growing process. In addition, as shown in Fig. S3, both raw LDHs and CQDs/LDHs displayed a type IIa isotherm and type H3 hysteresis loop of nitrogen adsorption–desorption profile (Fig. S3), indicating the formation of the mesoporous structure with slit-shaped pores [27,28]. The pore-size distribution results reveal that 3D porous CQDs/LDHs shows higher pore size to be 28.9 nm, which is two times bigger than raw LDHs. Those results proved that the introduction of the CQDs can not only build the 3D porous structure, but also improve the surface area, superior pore volume and mean pore diameter.

The FTIR spectrum of various LDHs was depicted in Fig. 4 . As the data shows, a strong and broad H-bonding stretching vibration of the OH centered at 3455 cm−1, which is due to the OH of the brucite-like layers and hydrogen-bonded interlayer water molecules [25]. The characteristic sharp absorption band at 1365 cm−1 is associated with the antisymmetric stretching mode of NO3 − [29]. The bands at 645 cm−1 and 418 cm−1 can be assigned to the vibration of O–Mg–O units in the layers. As shown in Fig. 4, the prepared CQDs/LDHs revealed strong absorption peaks at 3450 cm−1,1636 cm−1 and 903 cm−1 than the raw LDHs, implying some –OH, C=O and COC groups (oxygen-rich functional groups) were introduced by loading the CQDs. (Fig. S4) [30]. The FTIR spectrum of boron (III) free and boron (III) adsorbed CQDs/LDHs were also shown in Fig. 4. The FTIR spectrum also showed that the intensity of the –OH, C=O peaks of the CQDs/LDHs became weaker after adsorption of boron (III). The ν B-O vibration at 1413 cm−1 and δ B-OH vibration at 1120 cm−1 were both observed in the spectrum of boron (III) loaded CQDs/LDHs. Those results indicated the loading of B(III) on the CQDs/LDHs by formation of inner compounds (–OH bonds change into -O-B bonds) which is consistent with the literature information [31-33].

Table 3 lists the comparison of maximum boron (III) adsorption capacity of various adsorbents. It can be concluded that the 3D porous CQDs@LDHs prepared in this work showed relatively large adsorption capacity of 19.5 mg/g as compared to the literature. Thus, it is noteworthy that the considerable changes in the surface properties were achieved by a simple method, which should be attributed to the introduction of CQDs as the structure-directing agent.

The adsorption performance of boron (III) on the CQDs/LDHs and LDHs material was presented in Fig. 5 . As illustrated in Fig. 5a, the adsorption capacities of CQDs/LDHs and initial LDHs material were 19.5 mg/g and 7.49 mg/g, respectively. The maximum removal rate of CQDs/LDHs on boron (III) is 98% which is three times bigger than the LDHs. It might be due to the increase of the surface area and the functional groups of the 3D porous CQDs/LDHs. As shown in Fig 4, the synthesized biomass-based CQDs contain abundant of –OH and C=O groups and these groups are often located in the cis position called “Vis-diols”, which has a high selectivity to boron (III) [39].

In order to study the selective adsorption potential of the CQDs/LDHs and LDHs, the competitive adsorption experiment in the mix solution was investigated. After the adsorption process, the concentrations of the competitive ions (SO4 2−, PO4 3− and Cl−) were analyzed by ion chromatography (HIC-VP Super) and CO3 2− was analyzed by indirect titration, respectively. As shown in Fig 5b, CQDs/LDHs demonstrated higher selectivity than LDHs for boron (III) toward competitive ions. In addition, the static distribution coefficient (Kd ) and selectivity coefficient (K) computation formula is as follows: [40,41]
(2)
K d
=
q e
/
C e
(3)
K =
K d
[
M
n −
]
/
K d
[
N
n −
]
where qe (mg/g) is the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent, Ce is the equilibrium concentration, K is selective coefficient, Kd [Mn−] is the boron (III) distribution coefficient, Kd [Nn
−] is the competition ion distribution coefficient.

As shown in Table 4 , the competitive ions SO4 2−, CO3 2−, PO4 3− and Cl− core-exist, the removal rate of CQDs/LDHs toward boron (III) can reach 89.7%. Selectivity coefficients of boron (III)/SO4 2−, boron (III)/CO3 2−, boron (III)/PO4 3− and boron (III)/Cl− were 34.9, 43.9, 22.7 and 112.7, respectively. Both were greater than 1, which suggested that CQDs/LDHs show a potentially stronger selective adsorption capacity on boron (III). Compared with LDHs, the selectivity coefficients of boron (III)/SO4 2−, boron (III)/CO3 2−, boron (III)/PO4 3− and boron (III)/Cl− were 0.7, 1.0, 0.4 and 2.2, respectively, proving poorly selectivity of LDHs.

In order to have a better understanding of the correlation between the CQDs loading amount and the adsorption performance, the adsorption capacity of boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs at various loading amount of CQDs was investigated. It can be seen in Fig. 5c, the adsorption capacity of boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs is increased with the increasing of the initial CQDs loading amount from 0 to 3 ppm and reached the maximum adsorption at 3 ppm. The boron (III) adsorption capacity began to reduced when the CQDs loading amount up to 4 ppm. It could be that some functional adsorption sites were covered by the united CQDs. Therefore, in this study, the CQDs/LDHs with the CQDs loading content at 3 ppm were used as a potential adsorbent in the other boron (III) adsorption experiments.

The effect of the solution pH on the adsorption capacity of boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs is demonstrated in Fig. 5d. It can be observed that the adsorption capacity of CQDs/LDHs was significantly dependent on the solution pH. The adsorption capacity of CQDs/LDHs toward boron (III) increased when the pH was raised from 2 to 8, reached a maximum at pH of 8.5 and then decreased considerably by raising the pH from 8.5 to 12. This behavior is similar to the literature reports by Koilraj and Srinivasan [42] and Qiu et al. [43] for the adsorption of boron (III) on LDHs. It can be illustrated by the correlation of the surface charge of the CQDs/LDHs surface and boron (III) species with different pH solution.

The zeta potential (ZP) distribution of the LDHs and CQDs/LDHs as a function of the solution pH is plotted in Fig. 6 . The pHIEP values of the boron (III) free CQDs/LDHs and boron (III) loaded CQDs/LDHs were pHIEP = 8.54 and pHIEP = 7.27, respectively. The pHIEP value of CQDs/LDHs is decreased when the boron (III) adsorbed, indicates that the negatively charged boron (III) species were adsorbed on the surface of the CQDs/LDHs. Furthermore, according to the speciation diagram of H3BO3 [44], the predominant species is H3BO3 when pH changes from 2 to 6 and the molar distributions of the boron (III) species are as follows: about 90% H3BO3 and 10% B(OH)− 4 at pH = 8 and about 20% H3BO3 and 80% B(OH)− 4 at pH = 10. Therefore, the H3BO3 species was adsorbed on the CQDs/LDHs at pH values of 2–6, the HBO3 and B(OH)− 4 were adsorbed at pH values of 8–12. On the other hand, the surface of the CQDs/LDHs was positively charged for pH values less than the pHIEP value of 8.54 and negatively charged for pH values greater than pHIEP. Therefore, the maximum boron (III) adsorption capacity on CQDs/LDHs occurred at pH 8.5 due to the positive charge of the CQDs/LDHs and increase in B(OH)− 4 concentrations when the solution pH is at 2–8. With further increase in the solution pH to 10, the CQDs/LDHs became negatively charged and the B(OH)− 4 was repelled from the adsorbent surface by the electrostatic repulsion. Furthermore, at higher pH value, the concentration of hydroxyl ion (OH−) was raised to 100 times, which would compete with B(OH)− 4 for adsorption on the CQDs/LDHs adsorption sites. Therefore, adsorption capacity of the CQDs/LDHs on boron (III) increased with the increased pH from 2 to 8.5 and decreased at the higher pH.

Desorption ability of boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs is an important information to explain the deep adsorption mechanism of the boron (III) on the CQDs/LDHs. The desorption ability of boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs was analyzed by carrying out adsorption–desorption experiments. Desorption data obtained at pH = 8.5 (not shown) revealed that adsorption was not reversible at pH = 8.5 because at this pH the desorption data were not on the adsorption isotherm at pH = 8.5. Moreover, the adsorption of boron (III) at pH of 8.5 and the desorption at pH of 4 were revealed in Fig. 7 . The adsorption and desorption data points are labeled as A and D, respectively, and the number represents the run number. As shown in Fig. 7, in the low solution boron (III) concentration, some of the desorption data at pH = 4 were very close to the adsorption isotherm at pH = 4, suggesting that adsorption of boron (III) can occur as reversible mechanisms in the low boron (III) desorption conditions. However, when desorption conditions changed into high boron (III) solution, the desorption data at pH = 4 were far away from the adsorption isotherm at pH = 4. This result can be explained considering that the adsorption of boron (III) can occur by both reversible and irreversible mechanisms which depend on the desorption conditions. What's more, the regeneration experiments were also investigated and cycles results are shown in Fig. S5. The value of cycle 0 corresponds to the adsorption capacity of the freshly prepared CQDs/LDHs. As the data shows, adsorption performance of the CQDs/LDHs decreased with the increasing of the regeneration cycle number. However, this decrease was not much and about 90.5% of the boron (III) adsorption capacity was maintained after four regeneration tests. Details of desorption and regeneration of boron (III) prove that the CQDs/LDHs can be used repeatedly and easily regenerated via NaOH treatment.

The effect of initial boron (III) concentration and temperature on boron (III) adsorption by CQDs/LDHs composite was revealed in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8a, the adsorption capacity of boron (III) was increased sharply when the initial boron (III) concentration increased from 5 to 20 mg/L and the adsorption capacity also increased with the increase in the adsorption solution temperature from 283 K up to 318 K. The fitted adsorption isotherms of boron (III) at various temperatures with the solution pH 8.5 are presented in Table 5 . Fitted adsorption isotherms data show that the adsorption process can be described better by the Langmuir equation with well R 2 > 0.999. It is indicated that monolayer boron (III) adsorption onto the CQDs/LDHs and this adsorption process may be a chemical adsorption process. The fitted data also reveal that the maximum adsorption capacity (qmax ) for boron (III) increased from 0.222 mmol/g to 0.446 mmol/g when the temperature increased from 278 K to 318 K. It is proved that boron (III) adsorption on CQDs/LDHs is an endothermic process. The relationship of ln (q/c) versus 1/T and estimated enthalpy changes (∆H) as the function of adsorbed concentration for boron (III) was shown in Fig. 8b. As the data shows, the ln (q/c) versus 1/T is negative correlation and ∆H > 0, indicates that the boron (III) adsorption process is an endothermic process which consists of the Langmuir fitted data. The negative ∆Gθ values indicate that the boron (III) adsorption process is a spontaneous adsorption reaction.

The contact time to reach maximum adsorption is of great importance for studying the performance of adsorbents. Therefore, the effect of contact time on the adsorption of boron (III) onto the two adsorbents (LDHs and CQDs/LDHs) was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 5c. Adsorption kinetics was described by the pseudo-first order, pseudo-second order kinetics and intraparticle diffusion equation, and the results are presented in Table 6 . The adsorption results show that the adsorption equilibrium time was ca. 100 min for the two adsorbents. In addition, the adsorption process on LDHs and CQDs/LDHs was both described better by the pseudo-second order model with the higher correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.998, 0.999, respectively. The correlation coefficient of pseudo-second order model was 0.1792 L · mol−1· s−1 with qe of 20.61 mg/g. The apparent rate constants of CQDs/LDHs at 273, 298 and 308 K were 0.197, 0.259 and 0.733 g · mol−1· s−1, respectively. The Arrhenius plot of the rate constants was shown in Fig. 8c, which yields an apparent activation energy of 20.5 kJ/mol for boron (III) adsorption on CQDs/LDHs. It suggested that the boron (III) adsorption on CQDs/LDHs is a chemical adsorption process as the activation energy is much larger than 4.2 kJ/mol for the physical adsorption process [45-47].

To better under understand the adsorption mechanisms, the concentration of boron (III) and NO3 − in the adsorption solution was studied. As shown in Fig. 8d, with the decrease of boron (III), the concentration of NO3 − was slowly increased from 0 to 0.4 mg/L, suggesting a small amount of boron (III) was adsorbed by anion exchange between boron (III) and nitrate. What's more, the inter-layer space of gallery height of CQDs/LDHs was up to 0.78 nm when boron (III) adsorbed, which is bigger than that of LDHs (Fig. 2). It is demonstrated that some of the boron (III) species were intercalated in the interlayer space of the LDHs because of the molecular size of the boron (III) species being bigger than that of NO3 −. The anion exchange mechanism can be represented by the following reaction:
(4)
(
≡ A l
( M g )
+
)
N
O 3 −
+ B
( O H )
4 −
→ ≡ A l
( M g )
+
− B
( O H )
4 −
+ N
O 3 −

The above results mentioned that the adsorption of boron (III) on CQDs/LDHs is a macroscopic adsorption and thermodynamic process. However, it does not give the adsorption mechanisms in the inner-sphere complexes adsorption process. Therefore, the XPS spectra and the PL spectra of the boron (III) free CQDs/LDHs and boron (III) loaded CQDs/LDHs were both investigated. As shown in Fig. 9 , a strong PL emission peak is observed for the boron (III) free CQDs/LDHs, which is attributed to the special fluorescence characteristic of the introduced CQDs (Fig. S6). However, when the boron (III) is adsorbed, the PL emission peak becomes weak for a reason that the B(OH)− reacts with the CQDs/LDHs and produce other complexes. The biomass-basic CQDs reveal high fluorescence characteristic as it contains some polysaccharide fluorophores which have conjugate structures. The fluorescent quenching appears after boron (III) adsorbed on the CQDs/LDHs. Similar results were obtained by Gao et al., who assumed that the electron-donating B(OH)− groups can destroy the conjugation structures and decrease the fluorescence of the CQDs [48].

To further illustrate the inner adsorption mechanism, the XPS spectra was also studied. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , a new peak appeared and located at 195.51 eV which corresponded to the B 1S spectra, suggesting that boron (III) was adsorbed on the CQDs/LDHs. In order to understand the types of residual oxygen-containing functional groups clearly, O 1s and C 1s peaks of the samples were fitted. As the high-resolution XPS spectra shows, the boron (III)-free CQDs/LDHs sample had 93.6% –OH and 6.4% O2−, when the boron (III) loaded, O2− component peaks increased to 18.8% and –OH component peaks reduced to 81.2%. It implied that the tetrahedral B(OH)4 − species and the trigonal B(OH)3 species were adsorbed onto the CQDs/LDHs by liberating surface hydroxyls from H-bonding and the oxygen of the CQDs/LDHs surface directly. The main characteristic peak of CC centered at 284.5 eV, CO–R centered at 286.3 eV and the C=O centered at 289.0 eV indicates the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups [49]. In the fitted data, the peak of –COOH does not fit for the reason that the –COOH group can easily change into –COOR in the alkaline environment (the synthesis of LDHs process). As shown in Fig. 8, there are 31.2% of C=O groups and 13.6% of CO–R groups in the prepared CQDs/LDHs, suggesting the successful introduction of the oxygen-rich functional groups on the CQDs/LDHs. Moreover, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1S peaks also show that after boron (III) adsorption, the content of C=O group in CQDs/LDHs significantly decreased from 31.2% to 24.6%. In contrast, the content of CO groups increased from 13.6% to 20.3%. This suggested that 6.7% C=O groups are transferring electron and converting into CO group during the boron (III) adsorption process [50]. On the basic of above results, the possible adsorption mechanism can be expressed with the following reaction 5:
(5)

In conclusion, the 3D porous CQDs/LDHs have been synthesized by in-situ introduction of oxygen-rich CQDs as the structure-directing agent. The adsorption results shows that the synthesized mesoporous CQDs/LDHs exhibit favorable selective adsorption performance of boron (III) and its adsorption capacity was affected by the solution pH, temperature and initial concentration of boron (III), but no interfering effect by other ions in the mix solution. The fitted data illustrate that the adsorption behavior of boron (III) fits the Langmuir model and pseudo-second-order model. Macroscopic and microscopic data showed that the adsorption of boron (III) by CQDs/LDHs may be divided into two parts, including anion exchange adsorption between boron (III) and nitrate and the selective chemical adsorption between oxygen-rich functional groups of the CQDs/LDHs. This work provided a method for selective adsorption of boron (III) from aqueous solution, while the application of this method to other materials needs to be further studied in the future work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21677027), the NSFC-Xinjiang Unite Funding (U1403194) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21771029).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2017.12.008.
